segments.text: "Welcome to the Daily Droid, your source of up-to-date news and insights on the rapidly evolving world of robotics.
Today, July 24th, 2026, we've got six stories shaping the future of automation and artificial intelligence that you won't want to miss."
From The Robot Report, "Uber co-founder raises $1.7B for new robotics startup ATOMS,"
Uber co-founder and CEO Travis Kalanick has secured $1.7 billion in equity investment for his new robotics startup ATOMS.

This significant funding will enable the company to complete its "bits-to-atoms" story arc, focusing on building specialized robots for various industrial sectors such as food production, mining, and mobile robots.
Top investors including a16z, Ben Horowitz, Bain Capital, have contributed to this massive funding round.
Lead investor a16z is joining the company's board alongside other prominent partners, marking a significant boost to ATOMS' growth prospects.
With this substantial investment, ATOMS aims to further accelerate its development of advanced robotics solutions for various industries.
From The Robot Report, "Holiday Robotics raises $105M for its FRIDAY wheeled humanoid."
Holiday Robotics has secured $105 million in Series A funding to further develop and deploy its wheeled humanoid robot, dubbed FRIDAY.

This significant investment will enable the company to enhance its vision-language-skill approach, a full-stack system that integrates hardware, simulation, learning, and deployment, with the goal of increasing reliability.
By combining these elements, Holiday Robotics aims to create a robot that can safely work alongside humans in manufacturing environments.
The FRIDAY robot boasts a wheel-based mobile base, dexterous hands equipped with tactile sensing and force-aware control, and advanced perception systems.
This funding will support the company's efforts to deploy this technology and expand its capabilities.
From The Robot Report, "AMD unveils Kria module for real-time control, unified memory for robots,"
AMD has unveiled a new module designed for real-time control and unified memory in robots.

The Ryzen AI Embedded X100 series and Kria AI Robotics platform offer deterministic real-time control, as well as unified CPU-GPU-NPU memory that can be shared across the system, providing a potentially more efficient and scalable solution for robotics workloads.
The AMD team has developed a custom SoC designed to deliver high-performance compute and AI workload processing in physical robots.
The Kria module is built on an open software stack, allowing developers to create their own applications and integrations.

Compared to NVIDIA's architecture, the Ryzen AI Embedded X100 series can outperform on system-level robotics workloads, making it a compelling alternative for companies looking to build robots with advanced AI capabilities.
This development marks a significant step forward for robotics, potentially opening up new possibilities for industries such as manufacturing, healthcare, and logistics.

As AMD continues to refine its offerings in the AI space, investors and industry professionals should watch closely for future updates on this platform, which is expected to have a significant impact on the robotics landscape.
From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: Convert in the terminal,"
a free CLI tool that can convert CAD assemblies to URDF without requiring any CAD plugins is being considered for open-sourcing.

This development could enable users to work directly with ROS from their terminal using just one command, streamlining the conversion process and providing a more seamless integration between CAD software and ROS.
Researchers have been exploring this concept, examining how such a tool would be implemented and tested, as well as engaging with potential users through surveys.
Participants were asked if they would use a CLI-based conversion tool, with responses indicating that some users see benefits in using a command-line interface for such tasks.
However, others expressed concerns about the limitations of a text-based approach, citing existing CAD add-ins as an alternative.
The proposed open-sourcing of this tool could have significant implications for the robotics and AI industries, offering a more accessible entry point for developers and hobbyists alike.
As the project moves forward, users can expect to see updates on the development progress and potential release dates.
From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: Logging and Observability Guide Review Part 2 | Cloud Robotics WG Meeting 2026-08-24".
The Cloud Robotics Working Group continued refining the first draft of its Logging and Observability guide during a meeting on August 24, 2026.
After the previous session's unrecorded meeting due to lack of engaging video content, the group resumed editing the document, with contributions from participants shaping the final version.
The updated guide is now being reviewed, with an emphasis on clarity and consistency in logging best practices for cloud robotics applications.
The review process will ensure that the document accurately reflects industry standards and best practices.
From Robohub, "A mini robot to simplify dental treatment"

is a story about researchers at the University of Basel developing a miniature robot called "MIR" that precisely prepares teeth for a crown by measuring 43x26x28mm and fitting comfortably into an open mouth.

The MIR has demonstrated impressive precision in its current form, with positional error measured at less than 0.2 millimeters, a result set to be further improved upon once sensors are integrated into the system.

Researchers have conducted extensive testing on the robot's capabilities, showcasing its effectiveness in streamlining dental treatment procedures and reducing the need for multiple appointments with dentists.

The next step involves integrating a camera and monitoring equipment to enable real-time tracking of the robot's position and progress during treatment, paving the way for enhanced accuracy and efficiency in dental care.
"Thanks for gear-grinding with us today, July 24, 2026!
That's a huge boost for ATOMS - talk about batteries powering innovation!
Until tomorrow, when we'll be recharging the conversation, say goodbye and keep on robot-ing!"
